title: Model Evaluation Metrics Quiz
description: Test your knowledge on Accuracy, Precision, Recall, F1-Scores, and the Confusion Matrix.

questions.question: Why is simple 'Accuracy' a highly misleading and dangerous metric when evaluating a model trained on heavily imbalanced datasets (e.g., detecting a rare disease affecting 0.1% of patients)?

options.text: Because Python calculates Accuracy using integer division, losing decimal precision.
It isn't; Accuracy is always the gold standard.
Because a 'dumb' model that simply predicts 'Healthy' 100% of the time, without intelligently looking at any data, would technically achieve 99.9% Accuracy while completely failing its objective.
Because it requires manual calculus to compute.

questions.question: In a Confusion Matrix assessing a Cancer Detection AI, what represents a catastrophic 'False Negative'?

options.text: The model accurately predicting a patient is healthy.
The model incorrectly predicting a completely healthy patient has cancer, causing stress and requiring further testing.
The model incorrectly predicting a patient is healthy when they actually possess a dangerous, cancerous tumor, sending them home without treatment.
The model accurately detecting the tumor.

questions.question: If you are designing a rigorous Email Spam Filter, what classification metric should you optimize if your absolute highest priority is ensuring a legitimate 'Job Offer' email is never accidentally tossed into the Spam folder?

options.text: Recall
Gradient Slope
Mean Squared Error
Precision

questions.question: Which specific Machine Learning evaluation metric provides a balanced 'Harmonic Mean' between Precision and Recall, producing a single, highly reliable score?

options.text: The F1-Score
The Alpha-Beta Metric
R-Squared
Cohen's Kappa

questions.question: What is the primary statistical goal of performing rigorous 'k-Fold Cross-Validation' on your dataset prior to deploying a model to production?

options.text: To double the physical size of the training dataset using deepfakes.
To artificially inject missing null values.

To prove the model's robustness by sequentially rotating different 'chunks' of the dataset as the test set multiple times, ensuring the model didn't just get lucky on a single straightforward 80/20 data split.
To convert Python objects into TensorFlow execution graphs.
